title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. Bartleby's distant and ungrateful manner bothered me, even though I had treated him well. I planned to fire him when I arrived but oddly felt I shouldn't be harsh, as if that would make me a villain. I approached kindly, asking him to try to follow the office's norms.
B. Not only did there seem to lurk in it a certain calm disdain, but his perverseness seemed ungrateful, considering the undeniable good usage and indulgence he had received from me. Again I sat ruminating what I should do. Mortified as I was at his behavior, and resolved as I had been to dismiss him when I entered my offices, nevertheless I strangely felt something superstitious knocking at my heart, and forbidding me to carry out my purpose, and denouncing me for a villain if I dared to breathe one bitter word against this forlornest of mankind. At last, familiarly drawing my chair behind his screen, I sat down and said: Bartleby, never mind then about revealing your history; but let me entreat you, as a friend, to comply as far as may be with the usages of this office.
C. Although he seemed to have an attitude of indifference and ingratitude despite my good treatment of him, I felt conflicted about firing Bartleby as I had intended. Some part of me was warning against acting harshly, even though his behavior was unacceptable. I tried gently persuading him to comply with the expected duties. 
D. Bartleby appeared aloof and unappreciative, although I had been good to him. I meant to dismiss him but strangely felt superstitious, like some force was telling me not to react bitterly. So I gently asked him to try to conform to the office's standards, as a friend.
Answer:

options.A: Bartleby's distant and ungrateful manner bothered me, even though I had treated him well. I planned to fire him when I arrived but oddly felt I shouldn't be harsh, as if that would make me a villain. I approached kindly, asking him to try to follow the office's norms.
options.B: Not only did there seem to lurk in it a certain calm disdain, but his perverseness seemed ungrateful, considering the undeniable good usage and indulgence he had received from me. Again I sat ruminating what I should do. Mortified as I was at his behavior, and resolved as I had been to dismiss him when I entered my offices, nevertheless I strangely felt something superstitious knocking at my heart, and forbidding me to carry out my purpose, and denouncing me for a villain if I dared to breathe one bitter word against this forlornest of mankind. At last, familiarly drawing my chair behind his screen, I sat down and said: Bartleby, never mind then about revealing your history; but let me entreat you, as a friend, to comply as far as may be with the usages of this office.
options.C: Although he seemed to have an attitude of indifference and ingratitude despite my good treatment of him, I felt conflicted about firing Bartleby as I had intended. Some part of me was warning against acting harshly, even though his behavior was unacceptable. I tried gently persuading him to comply with the expected duties.
options.D: Bartleby appeared aloof and unappreciative, although I had been good to him. I meant to dismiss him but strangely felt superstitious, like some force was telling me not to react bitterly. So I gently asked him to try to conform to the office's standards, as a friend.
